content.text: What is Graceful Degradation?
Graceful degradation

is a resilience strategy in which a system, when parts of its infrastructure fail or become unavailable, continues to serve users at reduced capability rather than failing entirely. Instead of producing an error or crashing, the system falls back through a hierarchy of progressively simpler behaviors — each one less capable but still useful — until service is restored.

In agentic AI systems this principle is especially important because agents depend on multiple external services simultaneously: LLM inference providers, tool APIs, vector stores, databases, and message queues. Any of these can become slow, rate-limited, or completely unreachable. Without deliberate degradation logic, a single upstream outage cascades into a complete agent failure. With it, the agent continues operating — perhaps with shorter context, a cheaper model, or cached results — so that production pipelines do not halt.
Why it matters

LLM providers experience availability events — model endpoints go down, rate limits are hit, latency spikes occur. Agentic pipelines that run autonomously (scheduled jobs, document processors, multi-step reasoning workflows) may run for minutes or hours. If a transient API error 20 minutes into a 30-minute pipeline causes a complete failure and restart, all prior compute is wasted and the SLA is broken.
Graceful degradation decouples
availability
from
full-capability operation

. The pipeline stays available even when the best path is unavailable, and returns to full capability automatically when conditions recover. This is the difference between a 99.9% and a 99.0% production SLA on AI-powered services.
Benefits
Continued availability:
users and downstream systems receive a response — possibly with a disclaimer — instead of an error, even during upstream outages.
Cost efficiency:
falling back to a smaller, cheaper model during peak load or rate-limiting reduces spend without sacrificing availability. You pay for the most capable model only when it is reachable and necessary.
Transparent recovery:
circuit breakers and fallback tiers recover automatically without operator intervention, reducing on-call burden.
Partial correctness over total failure:
in many use cases a cached or simplified answer returned immediately is more valuable than a perfect answer returned 10 minutes later after multiple retries.
Observability hook:
every tier switch is a measurable event that surfaces infrastructure health to your monitoring stack, giving early warning of degraded dependencies before they cause outages.
Trade-offs & limitations
Quality loss is real:

a degraded response from a smaller model may be noticeably worse. Users or downstream systems need to handle or be informed of reduced quality. Silently returning degraded output without annotation can be misleading.
Cache staleness:
if the fallback serves a cached response, that response may be outdated. For time-sensitive queries (current prices, live status) a stale cache is potentially harmful.
Implementation complexity:
every fallback tier must be tested, monitored, and kept semantically compatible with the primary tier. The degradation logic itself can become a source of bugs.
False sense of health:
if degraded operation looks "fine" to end users, operators may not notice that the primary path has been broken for hours. Metrics and alerts for tier-switch events are mandatory.
Thundering herd on recovery:

when the primary path recovers, every circuit breaker may switch back simultaneously, sending a surge of traffic that re-triggers the outage. The half-open state and gradual recovery patterns address this.
How it works
Graceful degradation in agentic AI systems is built from three co-operating mechanisms:
Tiered execution paths:

each request is routed through a ranked sequence of handlers. The first capable handler is used; if it fails (timeout, HTTP 5xx, rate limit), execution falls through to the next tier. Typical tiers: primary model → smaller model → rules-based shortcut → cached response → static default.
Circuit breaker:

a stateful gate per dependency that transitions between CLOSED (healthy, pass through), OPEN (unhealthy, immediately fall back without attempting the call), and HALF-OPEN (probing — allow one test request to check if the dependency has recovered). This prevents retry storms from hammering a broken dependency.
Response annotation:
every response is tagged with the tier it came from (e.g. via an
X-Degradation-Tier
header or a field in the JSON body), enabling clients and downstream systems to decide how to handle reduced-quality responses.
Architecture overview

attrs.alt: Graceful degradation layered fallback: Incoming Request → Circuit Breaker → Primary / Degraded / Fallback paths → Response Collector → Client Response + cache update + metrics

content.text: The circuit breaker sits in front of all LLM or tool calls. When CLOSED, requests flow to the primary path (full model, all tools, full context). When error rates exceed a threshold, the breaker transitions to OPEN and routes directly to the degraded or fallback path without attempting the primary call. A HALF-OPEN probe periodically re-attempts the primary to check for recovery. Every path terminates in the same response collector, which annotates the response with its degradation tier before returning it to the client and updates the response cache.
When to use it
Production agentic pipelines
where downtime or pipeline stalls have user-facing or revenue consequences.
Multi-provider LLM setups
where you want automatic failover between Anthropic, OpenAI, and a self-hosted model if one endpoint is unreachable.
High-throughput or real-time services
where average latency budgets (say 2 seconds) cannot tolerate wait-for-retry approaches and a fast cached answer beats a slow correct one.
Cost-controlled deployments
where you want a cheap model to absorb traffic during low-importance periods (nights, weekends) and the premium model only at peak hours or for flagged high-value requests.
Tooling integrations with unreliable third-party APIs
— web search, code execution sandboxes, database connectors — where the tool failure should cause a simplified answer without tools rather than a broken pipeline.
How to implement
Define your tier hierarchy.

List every level of capability from most to least: e.g. Tier 0 = full model + all tools, Tier 1 = smaller model + core tools only, Tier 2 = smallest model + no tools, Tier 3 = cached response, Tier 4 = static default message.
Implement a circuit breaker per dependency.
Track success/failure counts in a sliding window. Open the breaker when the error rate exceeds a threshold (e.g. 50% over 20 calls). Close it again after a recovery timeout (e.g. 30 seconds).
Wrap each LLM call with try/except that maps exceptions to tier transitions.
Treat
RateLimitError
APIStatusError
(5xx), and
APITimeoutError
as transient; map each to either retry-within-tier or fall to next tier.
Populate and read from the response cache.
After every successful primary response, store the result keyed by a request hash with a TTL appropriate for your domain (e.g. 5 minutes for near-real-time, 24 hours for reference lookups).
Annotate every response with its degradation tier.
Add a field in the response body or an HTTP header so callers can distinguish a full response from a degraded one and decide whether to queue a retry for later.
Instrument each tier transition as a metric.
Emit a counter (e.g.
agent.degradation_tier{tier="1"}
) and set up an alert if non-zero tier traffic exceeds 5% of requests for more than 2 minutes.
Test each fallback path in CI.
Inject failures at each tier boundary and assert that the system falls through to the correct next tier and returns a valid response with the correct annotation.
Examples
Basic tiered fallback with circuit breaker
Context-window graceful degradation
A subtler form of graceful degradation: when the input is too large for the chosen model, truncate the context rather than raising an error.

import anthropic

MODEL_CONTEXT_LIMITS = {
    "claude-opus-4-5": 200_000,
    "claude-sonnet-4-5": 200_000,
    "claude-haiku-4-5": 200_000,
}

# Rough token estimate: 1 token ≈ 4 characters
def estimate_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return len(text) // 4


def call_with_context_degradation(
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
    prompt: str,
    documents: list[str],
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-5",
    max_response_tokens: int = 1024,
) -> dict:
    """
    Build the context from documents, dropping the last document
    if the total would exceed the model limit. Returns the response
    and how many documents were actually included.
    """
    limit = MODEL_CONTEXT_LIMITS.get(model, 100_000)
    available_tokens = limit - estimate_tokens(prompt) - max_response_tokens - 500  # safety margin

    included_docs: list[str] = []
    used_tokens = 0
    for doc in documents:
        doc_tokens = estimate_tokens(doc)
        if used_tokens + doc_tokens > available_tokens:
            break  # drop this and all subsequent docs
        included_docs.append(doc)
        used_tokens += doc_tokens

    context = "\n\n---\n\n".join(included_docs)
    full_prompt = f"{context}\n\n{prompt}" if context else prompt

    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=max_response_tokens,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": full_prompt}],
    )

    return {
        "content": msg.content[0].text,
        "docs_included": len(included_docs),
        "docs_total": len(documents),
        "context_degraded": len(included_docs) < len(documents),
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    docs = ["Document A content..." * 500, "Document B content..." * 500, "Document C content..." * 500]
    result = call_with_context_degradation(
        client,
        prompt="Summarise these documents.",
        documents=docs,
    )
    print(f"Context degraded: {result['context_degraded']}")
    print(f"Docs used: {result['docs_included']} / {result['docs_total']}")
    print(result["content"])
Real-world example
Scenario:

a customer-support agent answers queries by searching a knowledge base (vector store) and generating a response with the claude-opus model. The vector store becomes unreachable due to a network partition. Without graceful degradation, every query fails. With it, the agent falls back to answering from the model's general knowledge alone, returning a response marked as "best-effort — knowledge base unavailable". When the vector store recovers, the circuit breaker closes and full capability is restored automatically.

import anthropic
from typing import Optional

# Simulate a vector store client that can go down
class VectorStore:
    def __init__(self, available: bool = True):
        self.available = available

    def search(self, query: str) -> list[str]:
        if not self.available:
            raise ConnectionError("Vector store unreachable")
        # In production this would call your actual vector DB
        return [
            f"Relevant document for '{query}': Our return policy is 30 days.",
            f"Relevant document for '{query}': Contact support at help@example.com.",
        ]


def support_agent(
    query: str,
    vector_store: VectorStore,
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
) -> dict:
    # Attempt to retrieve context from the vector store
    context_chunks: list[str] = []
    knowledge_base_available = False

    try:
        context_chunks = vector_store.search(query)
        knowledge_base_available = True
    except ConnectionError as exc:
        print(f"[degradation] Knowledge base unavailable: {exc}")

    # Build prompt based on what is available
    if knowledge_base_available:
        context_block = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in context_chunks)
        system_prompt = (
            "You are a customer support agent. Use the provided knowledge base excerpts "
            "to answer the customer's question accurately.\n\n"
            f"Knowledge base:\n{context_block}"
        )
        degradation_note = None
    else:
        system_prompt = (
            "You are a customer support agent. The knowledge base is currently unavailable. "
            "Answer from your general knowledge and advise the customer to contact support directly "
            "if they need a precise answer."
        )
        degradation_note = "Knowledge base unavailable — this is a best-effort response."

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",   # cheaper model since this is already degraded
        max_tokens=512,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}],
    )

    return {
        "answer": response.content[0].text,
        "knowledge_base_used": knowledge_base_available,
        "degradation_note": degradation_note,
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Simulate outage
    vs_down = VectorStore(available=False)
    result = support_agent("What is your return policy?", vs_down, client)
    print("--- DEGRADED ---")
    print(result["answer"])
    if result["degradation_note"]:
        print(f"Note: {result['degradation_note']}")

    # Simulate recovery
    vs_up = VectorStore(available=True)
    result = support_agent("What is your return policy?", vs_up, client)
    print("--- FULL CAPABILITY ---")
    print(result["answer"])
Common mistakes
Not annotating degraded responses:
returning a tier-2 response with no indication that it came from the cache lets downstream systems treat it as a fresh, authoritative answer.
Retrying indefinitely before falling back:
three retries with exponential backoff is reasonable. Eight retries before falling to tier-1 adds 30+ seconds of latency and worsens the user experience.
A shared circuit breaker for all request types:
a single failed tool call should not open the breaker for LLM calls. Keep separate circuit breakers per dependency.
No TTL on the response cache:
a cache with no expiry serves stale data indefinitely after recovery. Always set a TTL proportional to how quickly the underlying data changes.
Failing to test fallback paths:
fallback logic that is never tested routinely breaks silently. If a code change alters the fallback response format, you will not know until a real outage exercises it.
Using graceful degradation as a substitute for fixing reliability:
degradation is a safety net, not a license to ignore upstream SLAs. If the primary path is in degraded mode 20% of the time, the underlying reliability problem needs to be fixed.
Best practices
Keep fallback paths semantically compatible:
tier-1 and tier-2 responses should have the same output schema as tier-0. A downstream caller should not need conditional logic to parse the shape of the response.
Set the recovery probe interval conservatively:
in HALF-OPEN state, send a single test request. If it succeeds, close the breaker and resume normal routing. Do not immediately send all traffic back.
Use per-request timeouts shorter than your overall SLA:
if your SLA is 5 seconds, set the primary LLM call timeout to 3 seconds so you have 2 seconds to attempt a fallback and still meet the deadline.
Always have a static-default tier:
even if the static response is just "Service temporarily unavailable, please try again in a few minutes", it is better than an unhandled exception propagating to the user.
Emit tier-switch events as structured log records:
include the tier number, the exception type that triggered the switch, and a request identifier so you can correlate degraded requests with upstream incident timelines.
Performance & scalability

The primary performance concern in a graceful degradation stack is that fallback logic must not itself introduce significant latency. A circuit breaker state check is a local in-memory operation taking microseconds. The cache read should be a local LRU cache or a fast Redis GET — not a database query. The degraded LLM call (e.g. to claude-haiku) is substantially faster than the primary (claude-opus): Haiku at 8k output tokens averages roughly 2–3x lower latency. This means tier-1 is not only cheaper but also faster, which can turn a latency-driven degradation (primary too slow) into a net benefit.

For high-throughput services, run circuit breakers per worker process rather than sharing state via Redis. Cross-process breaker synchronization adds a network round-trip to every decision. Accept that different workers may be in different breaker states briefly — this is safe because each worker eventually converges to the same observation of the upstream dependency's health.
Scalability note:

the response cache is the most important scalability lever. A hot cache serving tier-2 responses costs nearly zero in LLM API calls, making it possible to handle 10x traffic during an upstream outage from cache alone. Size the cache generously: most LLM responses for a given application cluster around a small set of semantically similar prompts.
Tooling & ecosystem
Tool / Library
Role
Notes
pybreaker
Python circuit breaker
Lightweight; decorator and context-manager API; configurable threshold and timeout
stamina
Retry + circuit breaker for async Python
Integrates with structlog; recommended for async agentic pipelines
tenacity
Retry library
Composable retry strategies; pairs well with a separate circuit breaker for tier control
Redis
Shared response cache
Use SETEX for TTL; JSON-serialize LLM responses; sub-millisecond GET latency
LiteLLM
Multi-provider LLM proxy
Built-in fallback model lists; routes to next provider on 429/5xx automatically
Prometheus + Grafana
Tier-switch metrics & dashboards
Emit a labeled counter per tier; alert when non-zero tier traffic exceeds threshold
Istio / Envoy
Mesh-level circuit breaking
Handles network-level breakers at the sidecar level; complements application-level logic
Comparison with alternatives
Strategy
Behavior on failure
Best when
Weakness
Graceful degradation
Serves reduced-quality response
User-facing services where availability > perfection
Implementation complexity; stale cache risk
Retry with backoff
Re-attempts the same call
Transient errors (network blip, brief rate limit)
Adds latency; worsens load on already-stressed dependency
Fail fast
Returns error immediately
Backend batch jobs where partial results are not meaningful
Poor user experience; loss of any partial work
Active-active multi-region
Routes to healthy region
Infrastructure can afford redundant deployments
Cost; does not help if the LLM API itself is the single point of failure
Queue and defer
Accepts request, processes later
Background jobs where latency is not user-facing
Does not help real-time users; queue can grow unbounded
Security & reliability
Graceful degradation introduces two security considerations. First, the response cache must be keyed and scoped correctly. If the cache key does not include the
user identity or tenant ID
, user A could receive a cached response generated for user B, leaking private data. Always include tenant and user context in the cache key.

Second, the static fallback message must not reveal internal system details (model names, internal error messages, stack traces). Return a generic user-facing message and log the full error internally.

From a reliability standpoint, the circuit breaker itself is a stateful component: if it gets stuck in OPEN state due to a bug, all traffic is permanently routed to degraded paths. Always implement a maximum open-duration after which the breaker forcibly transitions to HALF-OPEN and probes for recovery, even if the failure threshold has not been cleared.
Test the full degradation stack with
chaos engineering
— inject failures at every tier boundary in a staging environment to verify that the cascade is correct, that annotations are accurate, and that the circuit breaker recovers on schedule.
Key takeaways
Graceful degradation means defining a hierarchy of fallback behaviors in advance so the system stays available at reduced capability when dependencies fail, rather than failing completely.
Circuit breakers prevent retry storms by gating traffic away from a broken dependency immediately, allowing the dependency time to recover without being hammered.
Every degraded or cached response must be annotated with its tier so downstream systems can decide whether to accept it or queue a retry for full-quality results.

Context-window degradation — dropping documents when the input exceeds the model's limit — is a form of graceful degradation that prevents context-length errors at the cost of reduced input completeness.
Scope the response cache key to include tenant and user identity to prevent cross-user data leakage when degraded responses are served from cache.

Emit a counter metric for every tier switch and alert when degraded traffic exceeds a threshold — without this, a broken primary path can go unnoticed for hours while the system silently serves lower-quality responses.
Test every fallback path in CI and periodically with chaos engineering in staging; fallback logic that is only exercised during real outages accumulates silent bugs.
